body_txt: Grey or white hair in humans is usually a sign of advanced age (or of artificial bleaching)—but in fiction, even characters too young to have normal grey hair can sport it as a shorthand for past trauma. Often, a character's hair turns grey or white overnight (or even instantly in cartoonish media), as a result of a particularly traumatic event that changes their lives forever. This requires some Artistic License – Biology , as preexisting hair cannot turn grey without bleaching. It can only start off that way as new strands replace the ones that are naturally lost. However, stress can worsen hair loss and cause premature greying of new hairs over time. A mild form of Literally Apoplectic . Disease Bleach is a specific subtrope where the life-changing event is a life-threatening illness, injury, or another kind of bodily harm. Locked into Strangeness can also be a subtrope if the strange lock is grey. Compare Young Face, Old Eyes and contrast Prematurely Bald . To qualify for this trope, the character has to have some particular trauma in their life. Just being explicitly gray-haired without being old (such as Quicksilver or Kaworu Nagisa ) doesn't count.
